name: Gold Trail
name_original: Senda del Oro — circular desde San Antolín de Ibias
description: Hike the Senda del Oro, a moderate 4.6-mile circular trail from San Antolín de Ibias, exploring Roman gold mines and traditional villages.
region_intro: Hiking around Ibias offers diverse terrain, characterized by its mountainous landscape, dense forests, and deep river valleys. The region features ancient woodlands and varied elevations, providing a range of experiences for hikers. Its natural environment includes protected areas, making it suitable for exploring local flora and fauna. Best hiking trails around Ibias The most popular hiking route is Muniellos Lagoons Loop, a difficult 11.25 miles (18.1 km) trail that takes 7 hours 9 minutes to complete. This route explores a significant natural reserve, offering challenging ascents and remote landscapes. Another top favourite among local hikers is Small Oubachu route — circular from the Muniellos Interpretation Centre, a moderate 4.5 miles (7.2 km) path. This circular walk provides access to the natural beauty surrounding the interpretation center, suitable for a shorter outing. Local hikers also love the Gold Trail, a 4.6 miles (7.4 km) trail leading through historical landscapes, often completed in about 2 hours 29 minutes. This moderate route offers insights into the region's past while traversing varied terrain. Hiking around Ibias is defined by its dense woodlands, river valleys, and varied elevations. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate circular walks to more challenging multi-day hikes. The routes in Ibias are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 40 reviews. Nearly 400 hikers have used komoot to explore Ibias's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are available around Ibias?
A: Ibias offers a diverse network of over 10 hiking trails. These routes traverse the region's mountainous landscape, dense forests, and deep river valleys, providing options for various skill levels.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking in Ibias?
A: The best time for hiking in Ibias is generally from late spring to early autumn (May to October). During these months, the weather is milder and more stable, making for pleasant conditions to explore the region's natural beauty. Autumn also offers spectacular foliage in the ancient woodlands.

Q: Are there any easy hiking trails suitable for beginners or families in Ibias?
A: Yes, Ibias has a few easy hiking trails that are great for beginners or families. These routes typically feature gentler gradients and shorter distances, allowing everyone to enjoy the natural surroundings. For a moderate, family-friendly option, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1241881608/small-oubachu-route-circular-from-the-muniellos-interpretation-centre" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Small Oubachu route — circular from the Muniellos Interpretation Centre&lt;/a&gt;, which is a 7.2 km circular path.

Q: Can I find challenging multi-day hikes in Ibias?
A: Ibias's mountainous terrain and varied elevations lend themselves to more challenging hikes. While specific multi-day routes are not detailed here, trails like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1241945319/muniellos-lagoons-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Muniellos Lagoons Loop&lt;/a&gt; (18.1 km, difficult) offer a significant challenge that could be part of a longer hiking adventure when combined with other routes or overnight stays in the region.

Q: Are there circular walks available in Ibias?
A: Yes, circular walks are available in Ibias, offering convenient options for hikers who prefer to start and end at the same point. A popular choice is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1241881608/small-oubachu-route-circular-from-the-muniellos-interpretation-centre" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Small Oubachu route — circular from the Muniellos Interpretation Centre&lt;/a&gt;, a moderate 7.2 km loop.

Q: Are dogs allowed on the hiking trails in Ibias?
A: Generally, dogs are welcome on most hiking trails in Ibias, especially in non-protected areas. However, if you plan to hike in specific natural reserves like Muniellos, it's crucial to check local regulations regarding dog access, as restrictions may apply to protect wildlife. Always keep your dog on a leash and clean up after them.

Q: What kind of wildlife or plants might I see while hiking in Ibias?
A: Ibias is known for its rich biodiversity, especially within its protected areas and ancient woodlands. Hikers might encounter various species of birds, deer, and if very lucky, even brown bears. The flora includes diverse forest types, such as oak and beech, and a wide array of native plants, particularly vibrant in spring and autumn.

Q: Are there any hikes with great viewpoints in Ibias?
A: Yes, the mountainous landscape of Ibias offers several trails with rewarding viewpoints. You can find stunning vistas from locations such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4524571" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Acebo Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt;, or from mountain passes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5593878" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pozo de las Mujeres Muertas Pass&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5593872" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Connio Pass&lt;/a&gt;, which are often part of longer routes.

Q: Where can I find parking for hiking trailheads in Ibias?
A: Parking is typically available at or near the main trailheads and villages that serve as starting points for hikes in Ibias. For routes originating from specific visitor centers, like the Muniellos Interpretation Centre, dedicated parking facilities are usually provided. In smaller villages, street parking may be an option, but it's always advisable to arrive early, especially during peak season.

Q: Is public transport available to reach hiking areas in Ibias?
A: Public transport options to reach specific hiking trailheads in Ibias can be limited, as many routes are in more remote, rural areas. Buses connect some of the larger towns and villages within the region, but for direct access to many trailheads, a car is often the most convenient option. It's recommended to check local bus schedules in advance if relying on public transport.

Q: What are the winter hiking conditions like in Ibias?
A: Winter hiking in Ibias can be challenging due to its mountainous character. Higher elevations may experience snow and ice, making some trails impassable or requiring specialized equipment like snowshoes or crampons. Lower-lying areas might offer milder conditions but can still be wet and muddy. Always check weather forecasts and trail conditions before heading out in winter.

Q: What do other hikers say about the trails in Ibias?
A: The hiking trails in Ibias are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 40 reviews. Hikers often praise the region's unspoiled natural beauty, the tranquility of its ancient woodlands, and the variety of terrain that offers something for every skill level.

Q: Are there any historical or cultural points of interest along the trails in Ibias?
A: Yes, Ibias has a rich history that can be explored on its trails. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1239670030/gold-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Gold Trail&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 7.4 km route that leads through historical landscapes, offering insights into the region's past. You might also encounter traditional Asturian architecture and small, charming villages.
tips: San Antolín de Ibias is a parish in the Asturian council of Ibias. The San Antolín de Ibias Recreational Area is located in a natural environment on the banks of the Ibias River, pool and bar services are available between June 15 and September 15. It is worth visiting the nearby Palloza de Ibias Interpretation Center.
Village of houses with thick slate masonry walls, which we reach after climbing up the track between chestnut and pine trees.
If you deviate a few meters from the trail to the southwest, you will reach the top of the valley, where you can see the Roman ariferous clearings.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete the Senda del Oro circular hike?
faqs.answer: The Senda del Oro circular hike usually takes around 2.5 to 3 hours to complete. This duration can vary depending on your pace and how much time you spend exploring the villages and viewpoints along the way.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of this trail?
faqs.answer: Komoot classifies this trail as moderate. While it features a moderate length and elevation gain, many hikers find it to be on the easier side, suitable for a wide range of abilities. It includes easy stretches along the river and a winding ascent towards villages like Villamayor.

faqs.question: Where does the Senda del Oro trail start, and is there parking available?
faqs.answer: The trail starts and ends in San Antolín de Ibias. Parking is generally available in the village, making it convenient to access the trailhead.

faqs.question: Is the Senda del Oro trail dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Senda del Oro is generally dog-friendly. However, as it passes through traditional villages and natural areas, it's always recommended to keep your dog on a leash to ensure their safety and respect local wildlife and residents.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on the Senda del Oro?
faqs.answer: You can expect varied terrain on this route. There are easy, shaded stretches along the Ibias River, often lined with poplars. The trail also includes a winding ascent up a hillside, offering panoramic views, and traverses areas with historical Roman gold mining sites. The path is well-maintained and generally easy to navigate.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the route?
faqs.answer: Beyond the natural beauty, key points of interest include the charming traditional villages of Cecos (with a reformed medieval bridge and Romanesque church) and Villamayor. There's also a recommended detour to a viewpoint offering broader vistas of the Asturian countryside and the historical Roman gold mining sites.

faqs.question: Does the trail intersect with any other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, while primarily following the Senda del Oro, the route also passes through sections of the A Faena and the Ruta del Chao da Serra.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to hike the Senda del Oro?
faqs.answer: The trail can be enjoyed year-round. Spring and autumn offer pleasant temperatures and vibrant colors, while summer provides refreshing shaded sections along the river. Even in warmer weather, the riverside paths offer a cool escape. The pure air quality, indicated by abundant trees and lichen, is a constant feature.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required to hike this trail?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to hike the Senda del Oro. It is located within the Fuentes del Narcea, Degaña e Ibias Natural Park, which is freely accessible to the public.

faqs.question: Are there cafes or pubs near the Senda del Oro trailhead?
faqs.answer: Yes, San Antolín de Ibias, where the trail starts and ends, is a traditional village and typically offers local establishments like cafes or pubs where you can refresh yourself before or after your hike.

faqs.question: Is this trail suitable for families with children?
faqs.answer: Given its moderate difficulty and varied, engaging scenery, the Senda del Oro is generally considered suitable for families. The historical elements and traditional villages can also add an educational dimension for younger hikers.
